Assignment Policy in Robotic Mobile 
Fulfillment System Warehouse

智動化揀貨系統倉庫中不同產品組合下存貨單位之貨架指派策略
Product Mixture of SKU to Pod Assignment Policy in Robotic Mobile Fulfillment 
System Warehouse

iii 
 
ABSTRACT 
 
With the vast positive trend of e -commerce sales, the efficiency of warehouse 
operations needs to keep pace. Therefore, a part -to-picker warehouse named Robotic 
Mobile Fulfillment System Warehouse (RMFS) was explicitly designed e -commerce 
warehouse. This warehouse system can reduce the number of human operators and use 
robots, or in RMFS , called Automated Guided Vehicle s ( AGV), to carry the pods. 
There are several ways to increase the efficiency of the warehouse.

This research will 
focus on the decision problem, SKU to the Pod, or product assignment policy.  
This research has three scenarios: SKU to Pod using Random—baseline, Mixed 
Class, and Mixed Class Affinity policy. ABC classification and association rule using 
Weighted Support Count is applied to desig n the second and third scenarios. Then, 
using a simulation approach, those three scenarios are compared to find the best policy 
to increase warehouse

efficiency. It is indicated by looking at each policy's number of 
pods transported. The smaller the number  of pods, the higher pile -on the SKU to pod 
policy achieves. 
As a result, the last scenario yields the best pile -on with an average number of 
pods transported of 6.

59 pods per order . Meanwhile, the first and second scenarios' 
results are 7.06 and 6.90, respectively. From that numbers, it can be concluded that the 
pile-on of the last scenario is 7% and 5% higher than the other two scenarios, even 
though only 8% of SKUs form the association's rule . This result is verified by using 
one-way ANOVA. 
 
Keywords: RMFS, SKU to Pod Assignment Policy, Pile -On, ABC Classification, 
Association Rule 
  

 
iv

ance to let the

always helped and guided me to finish this thesis. Furthermore, thanks to my lab mates, 
especially Dinna as my topic partner, who accompanied me to finish my thesis, and 
Rasyid helped me with my simulation code. Lastly, thank my double -degree-student 
mates, Dea

1 
 
CHAPTER 1 
INTRODUCTION 
 
1.1 Background and Motivation 
Coronavirus has impacted the entire e -commerce world , changing  the nature of 
business. According to research, 52% of consume

rs avoid shopping in person and also 
avoid crowded areas. Furthermore, 36% avoid shopping in person until they get a 
coronavirus vaccine (Bhatti et al., 2020). Therefore, online shopping is one of the most 
popular online activities worldwide. In 2020, retail  e-c

ommerce sales worldwide  
reached US$4.28 trillion, and e-retail revenue is projected to grow to US$6 .4 trillion 
by 2024, as depicted in Figure 1.1 (Chevalier, 2021).  
 
Figure 1.1 E-commerce Sales Worldwide 
With the growth of e-commerce sales, the efficiency of warehouse operations also 
needs to keep pace. The Robotic Mobile Fulfillment System (RMFS) warehouse was 
designed specifically for e-commerce. This warehouse system can reduce the number 
of human

operators and use robots or RMFS, usually called AGV (Automated Guided 
Vehicles), to carry the shelves, called pods, to the picking stations instead  
(Merschformann et al., 2019). This type of warehouse can also be categorized as a part-
to-picker warehouse because the parts, or p roducts, are moved to the picking stations 
then the operator collects the products (Murray, 2019). In the entire warehouse, order 
0

Billion US Dollar
Year
E-commerceSales Worldwide

 
2 
 
picking activity is usually the most time -consuming compared t o others  (Frazelle, 
2016). Therefore, increasing the efficiency of order picking also increases warehouse 
efficiency. 
There are several ways to increase the efficiency of the warehouse. It is divided 
into three levels of decision problems. These are the strategic, tactical, and operational 
levels (Merschformann et al., 201

9) . Product assigning is a strategic decision that 
affects order-picking efficiency (Li et al., 2020) . Therefore, des igning a good policy 
for product assignment is vital to achieving order-picking efficiency(Silva et al., 2022). 
Product assigning itself has three decision problems. Those are products (SKU) to pod, 
pods to zones, and product dispersion over multiple pods (Mirzaei et al., 2022)

. This 
research will focus on the first decision problem, products to pod or product assignment 
policy. 
There are three popular product assignment policies which are random, dedicated, 
and class-based storage assignment policies (Gu et al.

, 2007). The random assignment 
policy is clear and straightforward; the SKU randomly assigns the Pod (Mirzaei et al., 
2022). On the other hand, a dedicated assignment policy is when each storage location 
is only used for one particular product. This policy will obtain optimal picking travel 
distance (Muppani & Adil, 2008) . Chan and Chan (2011) had a simulation between 
random and dedicated assignment policies. The result showed that these policies help 
to maximize the utilization of storage space and the system through

put , respectively. 
Using a random instead of dedicated policy might require a smaller warehouse but 
more effort to track inventor y accuratel y (Gu et al., 2007) . Class -based storage 
assignment policy assigns the products after it grouped into classes based on the order 
frequen

cy (Silva et al., 2022) . This policy with two or three classes can achieve the 
most advantages (Yuan et al., 2018) . The two advantages are significant cost savings 
and space sharing (Mirzaei et al.,

2021; Muppani & Adil, 2008). 
Other than those three popular policies, there  is another policy for product 
assignment called the cluster-based storage assignment policy. This policy clusters 
correlated items and then assign s the products into the Pod based on the cluster to 
minimize the inventory and material handling cost  (Kim, 1993). RMFS warehouses 

 
3 
 
can get advantages from clustering the frequent order together products on the same 
Pod (Mirzaei et al., 2021). Applying this policy saves order-picking efforts and reduces 
retrieval time significantly (Frazelle & Sharp, 1989; Mirzaei et al., 2021). 
Almost all cluster -based policy researches prove that storage assignment using 
cluster-based policy is be

tter than other policies based on their objectives. However, 
none of them are trying to find the best product mixture that could be applied in any 
other similar cases.  The product mixture is the optimal combination of the product 
clas

ses on pods that minimize the number of pods transported. The fewer pods that 
need to be transported, which may dec rease the number of AGVs needed, the higher 
pile-on is (Merschformann et al., 2019) . Pile -on is when the pods offer most units

necessary to fulfill the orders(Merschformann et al., 2019). 
An appropriate product classification also increases order-picking efficiency (Chan 
& Chan, 2011) . Generally, ABC classification is the most common ly

used in 
warehouse product classification based on order frequency. However, this 
classification usually solves the second de cision problem of product assignment: Pod 
to zones. Besides, they all use the one-class-one-pod rule, and none try to find the best 
product class mixture (Products to Pod) for each Pod. Therefore, this research aims to 
maximize pile-on by finding the best product mixture. 
1.2 Objective 
Based on the background previously described, the objectives of this research can 
be described as follows: 
1. To determine the optimal product mixture percentage within the warehouse by 
analyzing the various factors influencing warehouse space utilization. 
2. To establish the best policy for the SKU to pod decision problem by employing 
a simulation-based approach. This objective aims to determine the most suitable 
and effective strategy for allocating products to specific storage po ds, 
considering various constraints such as demand patterns, inventory levels, and 
available space. Furthermore, the simulation approach will enable us to analyze 
the performance of different strategies and determine the one that offers the best 
results. 

 
4 
 
1.3 Scope and Limitation 
The following limitations of the research must b

e acknowledged: 
3. The AGVs utilized in the research are capable of picking and replenishing  
activities. However, this does not consider the possibility of specialization of 
AGVs for specific  tasks, which may result in improved efficiency and 
productivity. 
4. The assumption in this research is that the pods will be directly replenished as 
soon as they become empty, thus ensuring that sufficient quantities of products 
are always available to fulfill customer orders.  
5. The dimensions of the SKUs are assumed to be uniform in this research. 
However, this may result in a simplified model that may not accurately reflect 
the diversity and complexity of real-world scenarios, where the dimensions and 
shapes of products may vary significantly. 
6.

The scope of this research is limited to the replenishment aspect of warehouse 
operations and does not consider the order-picking process. This means that the 
overall efficiency and productivity of the warehouse are not considered in this 
study. 
It is important to acknowledge these limitations to understand the research findings' 
limitations and the extent to which they can be applied in real -world scenarios. 
Nevertheless, the results of this research still provide v aluable insights into the 
optimization of warehouse operations and may inform future studies in

this area. 
1.4 Organization of Thesis 
The present study is structured into five distinct chapters, each addressing a specific 
aspect of the research. The organization of the study i

Chapter 1: Background and Motivation. This chapter provides the study's 
context, including the research's background and motivation. I n addition, i t 
outlines the research objectives and provides a clear overview of the structure 
of the study

Chapter 2: Literature Review. This chapter presents a comprehensive review of 
the existing literature related to the research topic. The literature review 
provides a comprehensive overview  of the state -of-the-art in the field, 
highlighting the key findings and contributions of previous studies. 
3.

Chapter 3: Research Methodology. In this chapter, the research methodology 
used in the study is described in detail. This includes a discussion of t he 
research design, data collection methods, and the methods used to analyze the 
data. In addition, t he chapter provides a clear and concise explanation of the 
steps taken to carry out the research and the procedures used to ensure the 
validity and reliability of the findings. 
4. Chapter 4: Data Processing and Results.

This chapter analyzes the data 
collected and presents the results of the study. In addition, the chapter provides 
a discussion of the result findings and statistical analyses. 
5.

Chapter 5: Conclusion and Future Work. This chapter summarizes the study's 
key findings and the implications of the research for future work. The chapter 
also includes suggestions for future resea

rch.  

 
6 
 
CHAPTER 2 
LITERATURE REVIEW 
 
2.1 Robotic Mobile Fulfillment System (RMFS) 
As e -commerce has become more prevalent, researchers  seek more efficient 
methods of managing warehouses and improving picking operations to reduce costs 
and inefficiencies  (Galliussi, 2022) . A robotic mobile fulfillment system (RMFS) 
warehouse is a solution for e -commerce sales growth because it fits many SKUs and 
demands multiple small-quantity orde

rs (Azadeh et al., 2019). In addition, this type of 
warehouse is introduced as a solution for automated storage/retrieval system (AS/RS) 
warehouse problems which are th e rigidity of changing inventory locations, the high 
cost of setup, and manual re-slotting (Enright & Wurman, 2011; Wurman et al., 2008). 
The solution offered by RMFS is robots that carry pods on the conventional 
warehouse called shelves, with items on the pods  (Enright & Wurman, 2011) . The 
robots carry one Pod for each robot from storage locations to replenishment or picking 
stations for later the pods being filled or picke

d by the workers (Kim et al., 2020). Each 
picking station receives a customer order when it arrives.  Then pods containing items 
in the order are  transported to the station by robots.  The operators remain at their 
stations because all items are carried by robots (Enright & Wurman, 2011). The RMFS 
layout is commonly arranged with storage zones of inventory pods, replenishment 
stations, and picking stations. The illustration can be seen in Figure 2.1.

There are several benefits of the RMFS warehouse. In 2016, it was found t hat 
RMFS's throughput is higher than AS/RS depending on the number of AGVs and SKUs 
(Beuters et al., 2016) . The throughput increases when the inventory is dispersed into 
multiple pods, the warehouse has an optimal number of stations, and the pods are 
replenished before empty Tessensohn et al. (2020). The other benefit is that the system 
can quickly adapt to new replenishment policies, and even if a robot breaks down, it 
will not affect productivity significantly (Enright & Wurman, 201

1). 

 
7 
 
 
Figure 2.1 RMFS Warehouse Layout (Kim et al., 2020) 
 
2.2 SKU to Pod Assignment 
Random, dedicated, and class-based storage assignment policies are three popular 
SKU-to-pod assignment policies. Besides these three popular policies, there is another 
policy for product assignment called cluster -based

storage assignment.  Using this 
policy, warehouse operation efficiency can be improved in several objectives, as seen 
in Table 2.1.

Li et al. (2020) aimed to not only minimize travel distance in the RMFS warehouse 
but also to minimize energy consumption by calculating the robot's energy 
consumption. Additionally, Mirzaei et al. (2021)  investigated cluster-based 
implementation in RMFS and ASRS warehouses. The research demonstrates that this 
policy significantly reduces time in order-picking activities compared to other poli

cies. 
2.3 ABC Classification 
Appropriate product classification  is also necessary to in crease order -picking 
efficiency. For example, in warehouses, ABC classification is most commonly used for 
racks or pod allocation with different objectives, as seen in Table 2.2. 
Table 2.2 ABC Classification Literature 
Year Authors Objective Warehouse

Muppani and Adil (2008)  and Yuan et al. (2018)  researched conventional and 
RMFS warehouses  to minimize travel distance for class racks allocation in a 
conventional warehouse and ABC pods assignment in an RMFS warehouse, 
respectively. Furthermore, Chan and Chan (2011) and Ang and Lim (2019) also studied 
rack allocation. The difference is that their objective is minimizing travel time in 

 
9 
 
conventional warehouses and minimiz e travel costs in unit -load warehouses. Finally, 
the latest research by Silva et al. (2022) could improve order-picking efficiency in the 
conventional warehouse by doing ABC zone sizing. 
2.4 Association Rule 
The study  conducted by  Yang (2022)  evaluated two association rule mining 
me

thods: the Jaccard Index and the Weighted Support Count (WSC). The Jaccard 
Index, developed by Swis s mathematician Paul Jaccard, is a well -known measure of 
similarity between sets that considers their similarities and differences. This method 
has been widely used in various fields, including information retrieval and data mining, 
to quantify the similar ity between different sets of items. The study also employs the 
Jaccard Index to measure the association between goods in a storage assignment study. 
On the other hand, the Weighted Support Count (WSC), which represents the 
relationship between any pair of  goods, incorporates the concepts of support and lift 
developed by Ming-Huang Chiang et al. (2014) . The study results show that the 
Weighted Support Count outperforms the Jaccard Index in terms o f accuracy and 
effectiveness, as Yang (2022) demonstrated. This comparative study provides valuable 
insights into selecting the most appropriate method for a specific data analysis problem 
and contributes to the ongoing research in association rule mining.  

 
10 
 
CHAPTER 3 
METHODOLOGY 
 
The steps outlined in this chapter serve as a comprehensive guide for the research 
process to ensure that it aligns with th

e stated objectives. In addition, a flowchart has 
been created, as depicted in Figure 3.1 , t o provide a visual representation of the 
research procedure. The flowchart presents a concise overview of the various stages of 
conducting the research and is a valuable refere

nce throughout the study. 
 
Figure 3.1 Research Flowchart 
3.1 Data Gathering 
This study's data collection generated 10,000 order data sets, each containing 5000 
unique SKU numbers. This generated data is achieved by analyzing 55,000 historical 
orders with the same total number of SKUs. The data analysis is performed using 
Microsoft Excel and the @RISK software add-in.  
In order to guarantee that the generated data accurately reflects the historical data, 
it is made to undergo the same statistical distribution analysis. As a result, t he inputs 
essential to each order data set are thoroughly list ed and explained in Table 3.1. This 
provides a complete understanding of the data used in this stu

dy and its connection to 
the research goals.

12 
 
3.2 Inventory Analysis 
Following the generation of new data orders, the subsequent step in the research 
process is the comprehensive analysis of inventory. T

his stage comprises two essential 
sub-processes, namely , SKU classification and the determination of the number of 
slots. SKU classification is a crucial component of inventory analysis, as it provides a 
clear unde rstanding of the types of products in stock. In addition, b y categorizing 
products into di

stinct groups, the researcher can analyze the stock more effectively and 
make informed decisions regarding inventory management. 
Determining the number of slots in Po ds is another crucial aspect of inventory 
analysis. This process involves calculating the num

ber of storage spaces required for 
each SKU in the Pod system to ensure that the inventory remains organized and 
efficiently managed.  
3.2.1 SKU Classification 
The SKU classification in this stage is performed by dividing the SKUs into three 
distinct classes based on their order frequency. The ABC rule is used, which results in 
10%, 30%, and 60% of the total SKUs being classified as class A, B, and C, 
respectively.  
Therefore, class A SKUs account for 60% of the cumulative ord

er frequency, class 
B SKUs account for 25%, and class C SKUs account for 15%. The Pareto chart of the 
ABC classification can be seen in Figure 3.2. This classification provides a visual 
representation of the relative importance of each SKU class and serves as a guide for 
inventory management decisions. 
 
Figure 3.2 Pareto Chart of ABC Classification 


 
13 
 
 
3.2.2 Number of Slots Calculation 
After class

ifying the SKU into three classes, the number of slots needed for each 
SKU must be determined. SKUs classified into one group will have the same number 
of slots needed. The minimum level inventory formula in Equation (1) is 
used(Radasanu, 2016) to determine the number of slots. Finding minimum stock aims 
to monitor inventory and reduce operational costs so that overstock does not occur. 
𝑀𝑖𝑛𝑖𝑚𝑢𝑚 𝐿𝑒𝑣𝑒𝑙 𝐼𝑛𝑣𝑒𝑛𝑡𝑜𝑟𝑦 = 𝑥̅𝐷 + (𝜎𝐷√𝐿. 𝑍𝑆𝐿) (1) 
Notation: 
𝑥 ̅_𝐷 = Demand Average 
𝜎_𝐷  = Demand Standard Deviation 
𝑍_𝑆𝐿= Z score of Service Level 
𝐿 = Lead Time 
3.3 SKU-to-Pod  Assignment Scenarios 
The SKU-to-Pod assignment in this study process is carried out in three distinct 
scenarios: random, mixed-class, and mixed-class affinity. The following sub-sections 
provide a comprehensive explanation of each scenario and its cha

racteristics.  
3.3.1 Random Assignment Scenario 
In the random scenario of the SKU-to-Pod assignment process, the SKUs are 
assigned to Pods randomly, taking into account the data order and inventory analysis 
conducted in the preceding step. However, it should be noted that this scenario operates 
within a set of guidelines, specifically that each SKU is dispersed across several Pods. 
This random scenario acts as a baseline for comparison with the other scenarios, 
serving as a starting point for further analysis. The results of the random scenario 
provide a foundation for determining the impact of different approaches to the SKU-
to-Pod assignment process 

 
14 
 
3.3.2 Mixed-Class Assignment Scenario 
The mixed-class assignment scenario requires the completion of three key steps. 
Firstly, the product mixture percentag e must be calculated using the principles of 
Independent Event probability as described in Equations (2) and (3). As per  Shafer 
(1996), independent events do not depend on one another for their occurrence. 
Therefore, this calculation determines the appropriate number of Pods for each class 
combination, as the orders between classes are not interdependent. Thus, the 
independent event probability is used to estab

lish the probability of each product 
mixture. In the second step, the SKU to Pod ratio must be calculated using Equation 
(4) to determine the number of slots required for each class assigned to a given Pod. 
Lastly, the SKUs are assigned based on their respective class. 
The results of these three steps provide a comprehensive analysis of the mixed -
class assignment scenario, allowing for a th orough evaluation of the impact of 
prioritizing high-frequency SKUs on the efficiency of the Pod system. Furthermore, 
the findings will inform decisions regarding the assignment of SKUs to Pods and 
optimize the utilization of the Pod system.

𝑃(𝑋 𝑎𝑛𝑑 𝑌 𝑛𝑜𝑡 𝑍)= 𝑃(𝑋 ∩ 𝑌 ∩ 𝑍^′ )= 𝑃(𝑋 ∩ 𝑌)− 𝑃(𝑋 ∩ 𝑌 ∩ 𝑍) (3) 
%𝑋𝑋𝑌 = %𝑆𝑙𝑜𝑡𝑋
%𝑆𝑙𝑜𝑡𝑋 + %𝑆𝑙𝑜𝑡𝑌
 (4) 
 
3.3.3 Mixed-Class-Affinity Assignment Scenario 
In the final SKU to pod assignment scenario , the process is an improvement over 
the previous mixed-class scenario. The first two steps, calculating the product mixture 
percentage and the SKU in pod ratio, are similar to the mixed -class scenario. The 
crucial difference lies in assigning SKUs to the Pod, which occurs based on the affinity 
between the items, as inferred from th

e order data history. 
Classifying SKUs before the association analysis is imperative, as numerous SKUs 
in the e -commerce warehouse can make the analysis challenging. By classifying the 
SKUs, the a ssociation rules analysi s is streamlined, hence the need to complete the 

 
15 
 
classification before the association analysis. The flowchart in Figure 3.3 provides a 
visual representation of the SKU assi

gnment to the Pod in this scenario.  
The third step of this scenario involves the analysis of association by utilizing a 
Weighted Support Count. This analysis uses three key metrics: support, confidence, 
and lift, which can be calculated using Equations (5), (6), and (7), respectively. T he 
support metric determines the strength of association between items, confidence 
illustrates the likelihood of certain SKUs being ordered together, and lift indicate

s the 
type of relationship between the SKUs. 
 
Figure 3.3 Mixed Class Affinity Scenario Flowchart 
𝑃(𝐴 ∪ 𝐵)= 𝑎/𝑁 (5) 
𝑃(𝐵│𝐴)= 𝑃(𝐴 ∪ 𝐵)/𝑃(𝐴)  (6) 


 
16 
 
〖𝐿𝑖𝑓𝑡〗_𝐴𝐵 = (𝑃(𝐵│𝐴))/𝑃(𝐵) = 𝑃(𝐴 ∪ 𝐵)/𝑃(𝐴)𝑃(𝐵)  (7) 
Notations and details: 
P = Probability 
𝑎 = frequency of SKU A and B are ordered together 
Lift > 1, complementary 
Lift = 1, independent 
Lift < 1, substitutive 
After finished the association analysis, the last step is assigning SKUs to the pods 
based on the first two steps. In this la

st step, there are several procedures to assign the 
SKU, those are: 
1. Assign the highest frequent SKUs on the highest percentage product mixture, 
2. Assign SKUs with the highest affinity with the assigned SKUs, 
3. Assign the next highest affinity SKUs of the next highest frequent SKUs 
assigned, 
4. Assign SKUs for the following product mixture.

3.4 Simulation 
In this study, the simulation of the three SKU-to-Pod assignment scenarios will be 
conducted using the NetLogo  software. The simulation will generate the number of 
pods visited for each scenario to evaluate the efficiency of the scenarios. This 
evaluation will be based on the number of pods visited and comparing them  with the 
other two scenarios. The objective is to determine which scenario produces the highest 
pile and hence, the most efficient storage allocation of the SKUs in th

e Pod system.  
3.4.1 Simulation Layout 
The design of the warehouse simulation in NetLogo is depicted in Figure 3.4. The 
simulation consists of three distinct sections: the picking station, the storage area, and 
the re

plenishment station. First, the picker awaits the arrival of AGVs carrying pods at 
the picking station . Next, the storage area is where the pods are stored, ready to be 
carried by AGVs. Finally, the replenishment station is where empty pods are refilled, 

 
17 
 
ensuring that the storage area remains fully stocked. The following are the key elements 
found in the storage area of the warehouse in NetLogo: 
1. Pod: A container where items are stored. 
2. Selected Pod: A Pod that has been chosen and matches the items with the 
assigned orders. 
3. AGV (Automated Guided Vehicle): A robot responsible for transporting pods 
to the picking and replenishment station. 
4. Aisle: The designated path for AGV to move  and access pods in the storage 
area. 
5. Empty Storage Location: An available space in the storage area where pods can 
be store

d.

3.4.3 Simulation Flowchart 
The simulation in NetLogo is illustrated through two flowcharts, Figures 3.5 and 
3.6. The first flowchart showcases the setup process of the

initial simulation, starting 

 
20 
 
from the layout, the replenishment process, and the order-picking process. Factors such 
as the warehouse's routing and replenishment setting, including the order, backlog 
order, SKU in Pod, and replenishment stations, are considered during the layout setting. 
Furthermore, the order -picking setting encompasses the order assignment, Pod 
location, and the location of the picking and replenishment stations. It is important to 
note that the input data for all three scenarios is the same, exce

pt for the item in pod 
data, which is determined through the SKU to Pod assignment scenarios discussed in 
subchapter 3.3. 
The second flowchart in Figure 3.6 showcases the functioning of the entire 
simulation following the completion of the initialization processes. The sim

ulation 
operates in accordance with the order data, both new and backlog order data, and 
comprises several steps, including: 
1. All data are input for the initialization process, including setting the parameter 
on NetLogo, 
2. NetLogo read the data, 
3. If there is an order available, then assign the order to the Pod . Otherwise, the 
simulation will stop, 
4. The nearest Pod containing the SKUs ordered is chosen

, 
5. The AGV is chosen randomly , and the AGV status on the simulation is 
changed, 
6. The AGV chosen is moving to get the Pod chosen, 
7. Move the Pod to the picking station. 
8. Update the destination and AGV status, 
9. Back to step 2. 
10.

If the pod status is empty, the Pod is chosen, 
11. The idle AGV is chosen randomly to get the Pod, 
12. Move the Pod to the replenishment station, 
13. Back to step 8.

3.5 Statistical Test 
In order to establish the validity of the simulation, it is imperative to perform 
statistical tests. The replication adequacy test is the initial step, which assesses whether 
the number of replications is sufficient to produce reliable results. Subsequently, an 
Analysis of Variance  (ANOVA) is conducted to validate the significant differences 
between the scenarios through a hypothesis test. These tests are critical to ensure the 
accuracy and credibility of the simulation results. 
3.5.1 Replication Adequacy Test 
The replication adequa cy test utilizes Equation s (8) and (9) as its mathematical 
formula, as described by Harrel et al. (2012).

Therefore, this test is crucial in ensuring 
that the replications conducted in the simulation are sufficient to produce accurate 
results.

3.5.2 Hypothesis Test 
After ensuring the number replication is sufficient, the next thing to do is a 
hypothesis test using One-Way ANOVA. This type of ANOVA addresses a simulation 
that has solely one factor. In this research, one factor is the policy of SKU to p od 

 
24 
 
assignment. ANOVA validate the simulation result by comparing the means of three 
scenarios and proving that those three scenarios' result is significantly different.

Based on the total frequency of orders, the 5000 SKUs can be classified into ABC 
classes. 10% of SKUs, which is 500 SKUs, that dominate 60% of total order frequency 
are categorized into class A. Class B is 1500 SKUs or 30% of total SKUs and 
contributes 30% of total order frequency. Mo reover, the SKUs that are minor 
contributors to the total order frequency are classified into class C. 
Different from determining ABC classification, the number of slots per SKU is 
based on the quantity of ord

er, not the frequency. Therefore, each class has different

ts, Equation

average number of units ordered per SKU is five, and more than 96% of the SKUs are 
ordered in le

ss than ten units on each order. With this assumption, the replenishment 


 
27 
 
number of each Pod will vary. Pods containing class C most likely have the highest 
number of replenishments, and pods containing class

A have the lowest number of 
replenishments.

4.3.1 Random Assignment Scenario 
In this study, 5000 SKUs were categorized into three classes: A, B, and C. The first 
500 SKUs were classified as Class A, the following 1500 SKUs as Class B, and the 
remaining 3000 SKUs as Class C. These classifications were made based on 
predetermined criteria. 
We randomly assigned the 5000 into 935 pods to en

sure a balanced distribution of 
SKUs. This random assignment aimed to prevent the clustering of similar SKUs and 
promote a more even distribution  throughout the warehouse. The 935 pods served as 
storage units for the 5000 SKUs, allowing for efficient organizati

on and retrieval of 
items in the warehouse. 
4.3.2 Mixed-Class Assignment Scenario 
In this scenario, we  analyzed 10000 orders and found that 9304 orders contained 
SKUs from Class A, while 7548 and 4627 o rders contained SKUs from Class B and 
Class C, respectively. To determine the likelihood of orders containing each class, the 
researchers divided the total orders of each class by the total number of orders. 
Having established the probability of orders containing each class, we then defined 
the mixed -class order probability using Equation (2). This probability was then 
converted into the product mixture percentage in the warehouse. Table 4.4 summarizes 
the probability of mixed -class orders and the correspon ding number of pods required 
for each mixture. This information can help determine  the optimal storage 
arrangements for the warehouse to ensure ef

ficient order picking and minimize the risk 
of stock shortages.

4.3.3 Mixed-Class Affinity Assignment Scenario 
To do this scenario, the first two steps, determining product mixture and slots 
percentage, are the same as the second. The difference is how the SKU is assigned to 
the Pod. The rules must be found in this scenario  using as

sociation analysis with the 
Google Colab tool. The result is only 8% of SKUs or equal to 400 SKUs that form the 
rules.  
Meanwhile, no rules were formed for most of the SKUs in Class B and C because 
the frequency of those SKUs ordered together is too low. Therefore, the 400 SKUs 
must be assigned close to each other while the rest are assigned randomly.

4.5 Statistical Test 
After getting the simulation result, the next thing to do is check whether the number 
of replication is sufficient. If it is insufficient, then the simulation needs to be replicated 
more. On the

other hand, if it is already sufficient, then the result is verified using One-
Way ANOVA. 
4.5.1 Replication Adequacy Test 
Due to limited time, there are only ten replications completed. Therefore, to prove 
that the ten replications are enough, the replications adequacy test must be done using 
Equation (8). The value of t and Z can be found on the Students T distribution table in 
Appendix 2 and Z table in Appendix 1, given that the confidence level is 95%. 
𝑒 = (2.262)0.096/√10 = 0.069 
𝑛^′ = ((1.96 𝑥 0.096)/0.069)^2 = 7.51 ~ 8 𝑟𝑒𝑝𝑙𝑖𝑐𝑎𝑡𝑖𝑜𝑛𝑠 
With the mean and standard deviation of the simulation result, the r eplications 
need

ed are at least eight times those. Therefore, the ten replications done are sufficient 
for this research. 
4.5.2 ANOVA 
The simulation results must be validated to ensure their reliability and accuracy. 
This validation process involves performing a one -way ANOVA after ensuring an 
adequate number of replications. The hypothesis test, shown in Figure 4. 6, is the 
foundation for this validation. 
A one-way factor ANOVA is a statistical technique used to determine if there are 
significant differences between the means of three or more groups. The groups are 
referred to as "scenarios." The goal of the ANOVA test is to establish if the differences 
in the simulation results of the three scenarios are statistically significant or if they may 
have occurred

by chance. 

 
36 
 
The p-value, which measures the strength of evidence against the null hypothesis, 
is an important factor in the hypothesis test. The null hypothesis assumes that there are 
no significant differences b

etween the scenarios. A p-value of less than 0.05 is widely 
accepted as statistically significant, meaning there is less than a 5% probability that the 
results could have occurred by chance. 
In this instance, the p -value of 0.000000182 is significantly less than 0.05, 
indicating strong evidence against the null hypothesis. As a result, we can reject the 
null hypothesis and conclude that the three scenarios are significantly different. 
 
Figure 4.6 One-Way ANOVA Result 
  


 
37 
 
CHAPTER 5  
CONCLUSION 
 
5.1 Conclusion 
There are several ways

to increase the efficiency of the RMFS warehouse. One of 
the decision problems is the SKU to Pod assignment policy. Although the d ifferent 
policy makes different warehouse performances, the policy with the lowest number of 
transported pods  is th

e best to achieve the highest pile -on in the warehouse. This 
research tests three policies as a scenario: Random—baseline, Mixed class, and Mixed 
Class Affinity policy. Each policy has a different rule for its product mixture.  
The simulation result shows that the last scenario yields the best pile -on with a n 
average number of pods transported of 6.63 pods pe r or

der. Meanwhile, the first and 
second scenarios' results are 7.08 and 6.84, respectively. From that numbers, it can be 
concluded that the pile-on of the last scenario is 6.4% and 3.02% higher than the other 
two scenarios, even though only 8% of SKUs for m the association's rule. However, it 
must be highlighted that 8% of SKUs dominate 55% of the order data. 
The result is verified by using one -way ANOVA. The p -value for the three 
scenarios is less than 0.05 , which means those scenarios are significantly di fferent. 
Therefore, it can be concluded that the SKU-to-pod scenarios have affected the RMFS 
warehouse's efficiency. The mixed-class affinity policy is verified as the best compared 
to random and mixed-class policies.  
5.2 Recommendation and Future Research 
 Before deciding what the best SKU to Pod assignment policy to be 
implemented in the actual warehouse is , things need to be considered the most : the 
number of SKUs that associate with each other and the contribution of these SKUs to 
the total orde r. Suppose the contribution of associated SKUs is high. In that case,  
implementing the Mixed Class Affinity assignment policy might fit be tter than other 
policies to maximize pile-on. 
 

 
38 
 
For future research, there are still many things for the RMFS warehouse  that need 
to be researched deeper. For example, from the side of product mixture in SKU to Pod, 
considering the quantities and dimensions of each SKU can be the next research 
opportunity. Because in this research, both things are made uniform. At the same time, 
quantities and dimensions should affect the SKU to pod assignment.
